title: Here Is Son
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at his home in Pelham, Massachusetts, in August 2013, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, a self-described humble servant of the truth, opens with his legendary father, a six-foot-four Jamaican who in 1930s and 1940s America was a footballer, motorcycle racer, and trick rider, known in Harlem as Son.

title: An Imperious Mother
summary: Thelwell recounts his father returning to Jamaica, winning election, and dying at forty-four amid rumors of political conspiracy, and the imperious mother who, left with tangled litigation, raised three children and once flew to the University of Michigan with a folder of clippings to secure her athlete son's scholarship herself.

title: He Told Them to Kiss His Behind
summary: He tells how his brother, advised to major in physical education, instead gave back the scholarship, worked his way through Michigan, and earned three engineering degrees, and how that brother, born three and a half pounds and fed by eyedropper, grew to six-foot-five and built Jamaica's department of natural resources before dying prematurely.

title: A Dread of the South
summary: Thelwell describes the deep dread of the South he had absorbed from John Oliver Killens's Youngblood, Frank Yerby's romances, and Faulkner, the petrifying first sight of Spanish moss in Louisiana, and his arrival at Howard in September 1959, which, he jokes, touched off the Southern student movement that spring.

title: The Best Decision for the Wrong Reasons
summary: He gives his official answer for choosing Howard, immersion in Black culture, and his real one, an elegant economist's tip that the campus had nine women to every man, and calls it the best decision anyone ever made for the wrong reasons.

title: An Organization That Did Not Exist
summary: Thelwell explains that the Nonviolent Action Group was never a recognized student organization, and only long afterward grasped that President Mordecai Johnson was protecting them, able to tell the Dixiecrats who controlled Howard's budget that no such group existed, while the annual fight for recognition gave the students a ready-made issue to organize around.

title: A Moral Obligation
summary: He recounts how NAG, sensing a moral obligation to support the South from the nation's capital, ran sit-ins in segregated Maryland and Virginia and picketed the White House, drew on socially focused professors and contemporary political readings, and on deep-South students like Cleveland Sellers, sent to Howard for safety only to plunge straight into the movement.

title: He Educated and Terrified Me
summary: Thelwell remembers Hank Thomas, who gave up a football scholarship for the movement, and Lavaughn of Birmingham, a veteran in the next room who first told the freshman just off the plane about the rural South, the Tuskegee professors who drove in chauffeurs' caps to avoid the patrol, and a police chief named Bull Connor, a name he would understand in 1963.

title: The Stick of Alabama
summary: He describes the cafeteria sticker shock for Caribbean students, his own poverty on thirty dollars a month from his mother, cutting hair in the dorm, and Lavaughn buying him a steak with the out-of-state tuition stipend Alabama paid Black students to study elsewhere, gifts from what he wryly called the stick of Alabama.

title: A Hired Thinker
summary: Thelwell honors the professors segregation kept at Howard, Eugene Clay Holmes, who told HUAC he was a hired thinker, Sterling Brown, E. Franklin Frazier, whose reading and discussion programs instilled social responsibility, and the NAG cohort they shaped, Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, Stokely Carmichael, Muriel Tillinghast, and the brilliant student-government head Tim Jenkins.

title: A Lion Met a Lion
summary: He evokes the heady international ambience of Howard in the capital, the African and Caribbean embassies the students could visit, a school that had trained much of the Black diaspora's professional class, and visiting figures like the brother of Tom Mboya and Kenneth Kaunda of Zambia, introduced with the line that a lion met a lion in the forest and the lion blinked.

title: Forty People
summary: Thelwell recalls busing a hundred Howard students to White House and CORE pickets and the demonstration over the new men's gymnasium, and his surprise on researching Carmichael's book to learn that NAG's membership across four years never exceeded forty people, who out of a student body of eight thousand shaped history in no small way.

title: An Area of Jurisdiction
summary: Thelwell describes the women of NAG as faithful, committed, and hard-working, never shy in policy debates or on the picket line but never pushed into formal public leadership, in a division he likens to African spheres of authority, and the extraordinary price they paid that he recognized only in retrospect.

title: She Would Not Straighten Her Hair
summary: He recounts Mary Lovelace, who wore her hair natural and was nearly thrown out of the dorm until Dean Patricia Roberts Harris interceded against housemothers who thought a natural a disgrace, and Bill Mahoney's NAG essay that at Howard one was neither Black nor white but merely object, the administration struggling publicly while never expelling them.

title: Two Different Prices
summary: Thelwell contrasts the price the men paid, mostly in upwardly mobile careers, a resume-building contemporary who went to Harvard Law against Bill Mahoney dropping out to organize the South, with the subtler and wider price the women paid, a struggle against the very concept of what a Black woman should be.

title: He Walked Me Through It
summary: He relays Muriel Tillinghast's account of Stokely Carmichael intervening to win her a place on an NAACP trip the male officers had taken for themselves, and of being walked through her first action step by step, the way a generation with no radical training acquired political skills very fast.

title: A Strange-Looking White Boy
summary: Thelwell remembers Tom Kahn, a white socialist activist, gay, and a roommate of Bayard Rustin, whom he first met over a conversation about Faulkner in the dining hall, and his impression that Kahn had come to Howard on a deliberate socialist mission to guide the movement toward Rustin's politics of nonviolence and labor alliance.

title: Your Dress Has to Be Nonviolent
summary: He tells of returning from a factory job in a mature, posturing denim outfit and Tom Kahn warning that his very dress would invite violence at a demonstration, and of the pale blonde Cynthia Jones who kept walking beside him on Virginia picket lines, to his terror, while children jeered that they would marry and have polka-dot kids.

title: You Could Stage a Debate
summary: Thelwell explains that because the administration could not give controversial figures a platform before its Dixiecrat budget committee, NAG's Project Awareness presented them in debates with an opposing voice, so the chief Communist Party theoretician Herbert Aptheker debated a socialist, and Malcolm X had to be set against Bayard Rustin.

title: Before You Were an American
summary: He recalls Rustin speaking briefly for integration and labor alliance before yielding his time, and Malcolm launching into a nationalist argument that before they were Americans or Republicans they were Black, to an audience of young Black people from across the diaspora, including Caribbean and African students who supported the movement but could not demonstrate for fear of deportation.

title: The Tried Red
summary: As the student editor sent to interview Malcolm, Thelwell waited at the girls' dorm with Courtland Cox, embarrassed when white co-eds from the demonstrations ran up calling out that Malcolm was here, and indignantly declared that no minister was going to tell him whom to associate with, mispronouncing the name, until he turned to find Malcolm X himself standing behind them.

title: Kittens in an Oven
summary: Thelwell recounts lobbing softball questions and Malcolm's answers in parable, that a cat having kittens in an oven does not make them muffins, that he would not fight for the wolves against the bears, an African manner of speaking in fables like Aesop, whom Malcolm knew to have been African, and a later demonstration carrying a coffin to Robert Kennedy's office where Malcolm watched from a lamppost.

title: As the Honorable Elijah Muhammad Teaches Us
summary: Thelwell describes how NAG liked Malcolm's militance and sheer dominating presence, so unlike King's, but perceived him trapped by the Nation of Islam's ideology, distancing himself by prefacing its strange racial theology with the phrase that the Honorable Elijah Muhammad teaches us, and looking wistful by the roadside as their integrated demonstration marched past.

title: The White Lady, She Had a Job
summary: He recalls inviting Malcolm to Friends World College after he left the Nation, Malcolm slipping into a Project Awareness event to hear James Baldwin, whom he respected, and a drunken older hustler on a Washington street corner who agreed with everything Malcolm said about the white man being the devil but would not give up his pork chops and insisted, to Malcolm's laughter, that the white woman had a job.

title: Interpose Your Body
summary: Thelwell explains that the men of NAG, mostly athletes raised on codes of honor, saw nonviolence as a counterintuitive strategy rather than a creed, and how the sticking point of what to do if a sister were attacked was resolved by the pacifist Tom Kahn's answer, that one should nonviolently interpose one's own body and take the blows.

title: Nonviolence Is Theatre
summary: He frames nonviolence as theatre and psychological warfare that controls the discourse and the image where there are witnesses and cameras, as in the demonstrations before the White House and Capitol, but argues there is no technique of nonviolence that protects against a sniper on a dark country road or a bomb thrown at a sleeping house.

title: The Master Race
summary: Thelwell recalls the American Nazi Party under George Lincoln Rockwell appearing at demonstrations in swastikas, the police forcing them their own picket line, and his irritation that these out-of-shape representatives of the master race taunted fit, clean-cut students, fueling a naive machismo wish to meet them on the walk back to campus.

title: Whether You Take a Weapon
summary: He recounts the long SNCC discussion sparked when a host farmer in Mississippi, defending his home against a feared Klan raid, offered Charlie Cobb and Mendy Samstein a weapon, and the resolution that struck him as eminently wise: the organization is nonviolent, but whether an individual takes up a weapon to help defend the home that shelters him is a personal decision SNCC has no business policing.

title: If You're Free, Act Free
summary: Thelwell tells of his first drive south with NAG, the feast at Cleveland Sellers's family home, and how, taunting the others to stop at the roadside restaurants they passed, he was called to make good on it, walking alone in the dead of night into a bright restaurant of sunburned Southerners and sitting petrified at a booth, served by no one, until he was grateful to flee back to the car.

title: My Grandmother's Beds
summary: He describes reaching the Black community of Atlanta and an immense rush of nostalgia at the little houses with raised flower beds edged in shells, the very flowers and arrangements of his grandmother's home in the hills of Westmoreland, Jamaica, a memory unvisited for decades that came rushing back.

title: They Needed a Gestetner
summary: Thelwell recounts James Forman flying him to Atlanta to turn a taped history of SNCC into a speech, the moving narrative of building the organization from nothing, the early project that needed only a duplicating machine no one could fund, and the poverty of a national office with one telephone paid for by Connie Curry and one room given by SCLC.

title: May the Circle Be Unbroken
summary: He describes shaping Forman's speech, which Forman wanted to counter Frank Smith's bid for the chairmanship, around the image of an unbroken circle, and quietly priming the audience the night before by going table to table among the singing Southern staff to ask them to sing May the Circle Be Unbroken.

title: Band by, Band by
summary: Thelwell recalls the UAW-discounted cars the staff loved drawn into the speech, Forman delivering it and turning from the microphone as King would, and the hall spontaneously rising in song, so moving that Thelwell, who had engineered it, had to run from the room in tears.

title: You Done Just Treated Me
summary: He tells how, fleeing the room in tears, he found the insurgent Frank Smith outside crying too, conceding he had been treated, and recalls another fracturing meeting saved when Jean Wheeler led the haunting Freedom Is a Constant Sorrow.

title: A Presence in Washington
summary: Graduated and at loose ends, Thelwell took the job NAG suggested as SNCC's Washington representative, the polite word for lobbyist, joined by Bill Mahoney, who arrived with a wooden box of philosophy books and a cardboard box holding his entire wardrobe, to run a conference teaching field and local people how to access federal programs.

title: Mrs. Hamer Began to Sing
summary: He remembers the federal-programs conference flagging until Bob called up Fannie Lou Hamer, who told how she went to register and then began to sing, the whole room joining in, Thelwell's first real encounter with the Southern spirit of the movement and the best of Southern Black culture, an occasion he never forgot.

title: Something to Trade
summary: Thelwell describes learning Black power in concrete terms while begging resources for the conference: that the movement could no longer be a supplicant but must bring something to trade, and Forman's lesson when the persona-non-grata Teamsters gave a thousand dollars, that you cannot sleep with someone at night and refuse to walk down the street with them in the morning, so the Teamsters topped the list of sponsors.

title: To Break the Logjam
summary: Thelwell recounts Bob Moses coming to Washington in the spring of 1964 with the decision, reached after the murder of Louis Allen, to bring Northern volunteers into Mississippi to break a movement stalemate, on the grim logic that the state suppressed the vote by killing the local leaders who could vote while sparing the outsiders who could not.

title: Joe Said We Could Be Seated
summary: He describes taking Bob Moses to progressive Washington circles where Moses unveiled the plan for a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party to challenge the regular delegation in Atlantic City, met with admiring doubt until Moses revealed that the powerful Democratic insider Joseph Rauh thought they could be seated, at which the room turned and commitments of support began.

title: The High School Friends of SNCC
summary: Thelwell recalls the office's work raising food and clothes for people thrown off the land, the suburban High School Friends of SNCC who collected and packed it, and the Hazard, Kentucky miners brought by a shared-office organizer, whose Elizabethan speech of high sheriffs and hollers he loved.

title: The Best Mess in Two Years
summary: He tells of taking the Hazard miners to a Washington Black restaurant where one wept over a Salisbury-steak dinner he called the best mess he had had in two years, and of loading their van with as much canned food as could be spared for Kentucky, a SNCC project there that never quite took hold.

title: My People and My People Struggle
summary: Thelwell describes a schoolteacher who came in and organized the rascally High School Friends, Ralph Featherstone, who soon decided to leave his marriage and teaching job for Mississippi, accepting Thelwell's warning that those who went down might not come back, and the well-dressed Jewish mothers who took over the office after Schwerner, Goodman, and Chaney vanished, getting through to congressmen on the phone.

title: Make You a Get Killed
summary: He recounts a mother kissing her son and sending him off to Mississippi with pride, his own mother threatening from Jamaica to set the FBI on him if he went, and how, when he finally reached a Mississippi meeting wanting to be a field organizer, Forman and the lawyers Kunstler and Kinoy turned him right around to go start the MFDP office in Washington.

title: Run Like Hell for the Door
summary: Thelwell tells of Featherstone bursting in proud as assistant director of the Freedom Schools, of meeting his best friend, a red-haired white Yale philosophy student who loved Mississippi, and of a celebratory dinner where, unable to cover a forty-five-dollar bill, the three-hundred-pound Lawrence Guyot solemnly proposed they leave the money and run before producing a churchman's wad of two-dollar bills, with Jan Goodman the seasoned political hand among them.

title: They Talked About the Victims
summary: Thelwell describes the propaganda that swept the Washington beltway once the summer project was known, that it was quixotic, cynical, and really a scheme to get young white Americans killed, a perverse framing that named the potential victims rather than the terrorists who would do the killing, pushed even by syndicated columnists like the Alsop brothers.

title: Disease, Castro, and Tear Gas
summary: He recounts the austere columnist Joseph Alsop claiming proof that SNCC meant to create martyrs, and the parallel campaign inside Mississippi casting the volunteers as disease-ridden, Castro-financed invaders, with special legislation passed, tanks and tear gas bought, and the Jackson papers' incendiary coverage stoking an atmosphere in which a young white Mississippian might think it his patriotic duty to stop them.

title: Get the Right Witnesses
summary: Thelwell explains his strategy of countering the suppressed story by getting liberal congressmen like William Fitts Ryan and Robert Kastenmeier to hold hearings the media could not ignore, and describes laying out for Kastenmeier, point by point, the gathering evidence of a coming bloodbath until the congressman's head sank to his desk.

title: If I Live, I'm Already Registered
summary: With Congress refusing official hearings, Thelwell rented the National Theatre and staged one with Mississippi witnesses, among them E.W. Steptoe, who, asked by a looming sheriff to state his business, answered that if he lived he was already registered to vote, and Hartman Turnbow; Norman Mailer graciously declined as a liability, and Thelwell's own Jamaican accent kept him from the convention floor.

title: I Would So Like to Handle You
summary: Sidelined from Atlantic City, Thelwell tells of a New York laundromat encounter with a character actress of many accents, and of literary patrons who took him up, an agent who had represented Richard Wright offering to handle his award-winning short fiction.

title: If You Were Less Full of Hatred
summary: He recounts the agent showing him a paternalistic letter Faulkner had written Richard Wright, that he might one day become an artist if only he were less full of hatred, a message of admission to the high table of letters on condition of surrender, and the patrons' plan to set the Jamaican newcomer up as a Black American novelist in a borrowed Italian apartment.

title: James Baldwin Is Finished
summary: Thelwell tells how the agent dismissed Baldwin's Blues for Mister Charlie and declared Baldwin finished and the field ready for a new Black writer, and how, repelled, he left for Washington and SNCC, recalling Forman relaying Baldwin's vow never to betray them if they never accepted the degraded definitions the republic had prepared.

title: Congress Is the Only Judge
summary: Sent back from a SNCC meeting to open the MFDP office, Thelwell explains the congressional-challenge strategy reaching back to Reconstruction: that Congress alone judges its seats, that only a defeated candidate may challenge, and that with the Democrats holding a huge majority the aim could not be to win the seats but to force the question.

title: A Freedom Election
summary: He lays out the protocol by which a congressman rising as the chamber is sworn in could challenge a Mississippi seat and trigger an investigation with subpoena power, and the problem that with no Black person able to reach the official ballot, the only candidates had to come from a freedom election with no official status, an uphill struggle made harder by the recriminations heaped on SNCC after Atlantic City.

title: Undying Loyalty to the Ticket
summary: Thelwell recounts Lawrence Guyot's subversive stroke after Atlantic City: running Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray, and Annie Devine in a freedom election and proclaiming undying loyalty to the Johnson-Humphrey ticket, so that the only Mississippians forthrightly supporting the Democrats were the disfranchised MFDP, even soliciting Lady Bird Johnson to campaign.

title: You Prepared It
summary: He describes Joseph Rauh's belief that only his patronage had gotten the MFDP into Atlantic City, when in truth the murders of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney had given them currency, and his own first conversation with Rauh, refusing a thousand-dollar bill for a brief the MFDP never ordered, which ended in an expletive and a slammed phone.

title: No Situation That Can't Be Fixed
summary: Thelwell tells of a Bill Cosby benefit at Howard that SNCC badly needed, threatened by an official who said the unregistered group would go to jail if it held the concert, and how, on advice, he called Joseph Rauh, who said he never saw a situation that could not be fixed and made the threat vanish, a favor Thelwell later suspected Rauh had staged to make him grateful.

title: The Establishment Group
summary: Representing both SNCC and the MFDP at the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights, where Clarence Mitchell and Joseph Rauh presided, Thelwell endured cold abuse, with only Episcopal Bishop Paul Moore greeting him warmly, until Robert Spike of the National Council of Churches, who had felt betrayed over Atlantic City, publicly reconsidered and became an ally again.

title: We Are Not Asking for the Seats
summary: He explains the MFDP's counter to the charge that Congress would never seat three people elected in an unofficial freedom vote: that they were not asking for the seats but for the seats to be vacated and new federally supervised elections held, given a mountain of evidence that forty percent of the state had been disfranchised.

title: A Crib in the Eaves
summary: Thelwell recounts the conventional wisdom that no congressman would raise the challenge, and finding William Fitts Ryan, a maverick liberal of such low standing his office was a crib in the eaves of the House building, while Speaker John McCormack, capable of not seeing a giant rise from the floor, posed the real danger of refusing to recognize Ryan.

title: You Got Me
summary: He describes the scheme to reach the forty-odd freshman Democrats swept in on Johnson's landslide, renting a room beside their orientation and handing out invitations, where an upstate New Yorker holding a seat no Democrat had won in decades figured he might as well do good, Patsy Mink saw no way to do otherwise, and John Conyers, weighing a promised Judiciary Committee seat, got Thelwell's blessing to follow his conscience.

title: One Worker Is Worth a Thousand Students
summary: Thelwell tells of writing the powerful Republican Thomas Curtis a long letter that won a noncommittal opening, and of two ill-fitting-suited electricians' union men who, cutting through the academic rhetoric of a strategy meeting, handed him a hundred-thousand-dollar check and named a Pennsylvania congressman they would deliver, teaching him that one class-conscious worker was worth a thousand students.

title: A Line of Silent People
summary: Thelwell describes lining the poor Black Mississippians the MFDP had brought to lobby along the platforms of the tunnels between the House office buildings and the Capitol, silent in their Sunday best, so that congressmen rounding the corner stopped short and grew self-conscious under their gaze, a powerful moment he believed moved some votes.

title: Sixty People Stood Up
summary: He recounts the vote day: Ryan rising to challenge the seats and, behind him, a wall of some sixty congressmen standing, too many for McCormack to overlook, forcing the Mississippians to stand aside and triggering a roll call in which a hundred forty-nine members voted to vacate the seats, a result beyond anything SNCC had expected, achieved without the established liberal hierarchy.

title: Across the Tracks
summary: Thelwell savors Joseph Rauh and his aide hurrying in afterward, unable to make the unexpected tally add up, and describes how the vote's momentum opened the deposition process, sending hundreds of volunteer lawyers to subpoena white Mississippi officials who, for once, had to come across the tracks into packed Black churches to be questioned by local people.

title: A Trial Balloon
summary: Thelwell recounts pressing for the voting provision left out of the Civil Rights Act, and the administration floating, on a folded envelope, a trial-balloon voting rights bill that would let the president send registrars only to counties where fewer than ten percent of Black citizens were registered, which Clarence Mitchell, relaying King's word, moved to accept as the discussion ended.

title: That Means Ninety Percent
summary: Thelwell objected that a ten-percent threshold meant tolerating ninety percent disfranchisement and gave the president a toothless stick rather than an obligation, and that the movement should take to the streets to create a climate for real legislation, a memorandum he sent, fatefully, on MFDP letterhead carrying the signatures of SNCC, CORE, King through Andrew Young, and the MFDP.

title: Then Selma Happened
summary: He describes the Leadership Conference falling silent for weeks after the memorandum, Clarence Mitchell suffering a heart attack over being called a traitor, Selma erupting by sheer coincidence during the hiatus, and Walter Reuther seizing the vacuum with a telegram whose first paragraph dangled a million-dollar fund to endow the movement's martyrs.

title: The Widow of Malcolm X
summary: Thelwell recounts Courtland Cox deflating Reuther's martyrs-endowment maneuver by asking whether the widow and daughters of Malcolm X would be beneficiaries and how it would be explained to the press, and the stiff dinner Reuther then sought at a Black restaurant, an offer of a job Thelwell did not want, noting that the Voting Rights Act of 1965 was rewritten after Selma to real effect, to which he credits his memorandum a slight contribution.

part: Jesse Harris, and a Way to UMass
title: Captain, I Don't Believe I Will
summary: Thelwell tells the story he made an epitaph in his book: the Parchman criminals who refused, even for liquor and easier time, to attack the arriving Freedom Riders, and the local seventeen-year-old Jesse Harris who walked into the white waiting room beside them, was sent alone to a county jail, and when whipped for not saying sir lay back down and dared the jailer to beat him more, later a SNCC project director who ran security for King before disappearing into addiction.

part: Jesse Harris, and a Way to UMass
title: By the Good Offices of the FBI
summary: He recounts reuniting with Jesse Harris at a book launch and showing him the printed passage that vindicated his story, and how he came to UMass by accident and the good offices of the FBI, two agents warning the foreign student to be in school by September or be personally deported, the SNCC lawyer Len Holt needling them about their favorite girlfriend J. Edgar Hoover, and a rescued fellowship letter from Sidney Kaplan.

part: Jesse Harris, and a Way to UMass
title: A Statement With Holes in It
summary: Thelwell closes on a phone call from Andrew Young about King's memorandum that instantly warmed a skeptical department, the freshman-football-team class he was assigned, and the Leadership Conference's first meeting back, where Bayard Rustin read a King statement that pointedly never said King's name had been forged, its last lines scratched out until there were holes in the paper, a principled vindication under pressure from the columnists who insisted King could never have signed so radical a document.
